title: Level 4: External Interrupts (INT0 & INT1)
aim: To use external hardware interrupts on the ATmega328P to pause the main program and execute an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
objective: Understand how interrupt vectors function in C (using ISR macros and AVR/interrupt.h), configure EICRA and EIMSK, and debounce interrupts in software.
theory:
What is an Interrupt?

An interrupt is an emergency signal from hardware that tells the CPU to immediately stop what it's doing (the main loop) and jump to a specific piece of code called an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).

Configuring Interrupts

In the ATmega328P, INT0 (Pin 2) and INT1 (Pin 3) are the primary external interrupt pins.

  1. EICRA (External Interrupt Control Register A): Configures exactly what triggers the interrupt (e.g., rising edge, falling edge, low level, or any logic change).
  2. EIMSK (External Interrupt Mask Register): Actually enables the specific interrupt pin you want to use (INT0 or INT1).
  3. sei(): The global interrupt enable macro. Even if you turn on the INT0 pin, nothing will happen until you execute sei(); to tell the CPU to accept all interrupts.

The Global Variable gotcha

Whenever a variable is written in an ISR and read in the main loop, it MUST be declared with the volatile keyword (e.g., volatile uint8_t flag = 0;). This tells the C compiler that the value can change without warning, preventing it from optimizing the variable out of existence.

pretest.question: Which macro MUST be executed to enable global interrupts?
pretest.explanation: sei() (Set Enable Interrupts) turns on the master switch for all interrupts in the ATmega.

pretest.question: What is the correct syntax to declare an Interrupt Service Routine for the pin INT0?
pretest.explanation: The avr/interrupt.h library uses the ISR() macro passing the hardcoded vector name (INT0_vect).

procedure: Include the <avr/interrupt.h> header at the top of your code.
Declare a volatile integer counter variable.
Wire a push button to Pin 2 (PD2 / INT0) and connect the other end to GND. Enable its internal pull-up in DDRD/PORTD.
Configure INT0 to trigger on a FALLING edge by writing to EICRA.
Enable the INT0 interrupt by setting bit 0 in EIMSK.
Enable global interrupts by calling sei();.
Write the ISR(INT0_vect) routine to increment the counter every time the button is pressed.
In the main loop, map the counter's value to LEDs on PORTB to visualize it (a binary counter).

posttest.question: Why shouldn't you place a delay(_delay_ms) inside an ISR?
options: Delays are not accurate in ISRs
It halts the entire system, preventing other code and interrupts from running
The compiler will throw an error
posttest.explanation: ISRs must execute as quickly as possible. During an ISR, global interrupts are disabled, so a long delay will freeze the microcontroller.

feedback: Outstanding! You have successfully stepped out of single-threaded polling and learned event-driven microcontroller programming using hardware interrupts.
